title: Oil Surges Past $87 on Dual-Front Escalation, China Crude Imports Hit Decade Low, and France Cuts 6.4 GW Nuclear
subhead: Brent jumped nearly 15% in a week as Black Sea attacks and renewed Hormuz disruption hit simultaneously, China's June crude imports plunged to the lowest since 2016, and France slashed 6.4 GW of nuclear output during a record heatwave — the energy market is being squeezed from every direction.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Rally 15% in a Week as Two Theaters Escalate Simultaneously
sections.body:
Brent crude surged past $87 per barrel on Tuesday, touching a one-month high and capping a nearly 15% rally since Friday, as military escalation in both the Middle East and the Black Sea converged to drive the sharpest weekly gain in months. WTI crude traded above $81/bbl, up 3.7% on the day.

US gasoline prices rose for the first time since May, with the national average hitting $3.86/gallon according to AAA data, up from $3.79 a week prior. GasBuddy reported that nearly 80% of US states saw retail price increases. The rally was powered by two simultaneous fronts: renewed US-Iran hostilities that have cut Strait of Hormuz traffic by roughly half, and Russia's drone strike on a commercial vessel in Odesa that killed five seafarers — one of the deadliest single strikes on commercial shipping since the Ukraine war began.

Ukraine simultaneously struck 15 Russian vessels overnight in the Sea of Azov and Taganrog Bay, bringing their total to over 105 ships targeted in an eight-day window. Major agricultural firm Kernel suspended operations at Chornomorsk ports. The dual-front escalation is the first time since the Iran war began that both major energy chokepoints — Hormuz and the Black Sea — have faced simultaneous military disruption.
sections.whyItMatters: Two active war zones are now simultaneously threatening energy shipping routes, which has not happened at this intensity before. The 15% weekly price spike signals markets are pricing in a sustained multi-theater disruption rather than a single-front event.

sections.heading: China's Crude Imports Crash to Decade Low as Hormuz Crisis Bites
sections.body:
China's crude oil imports plunged 41.3% year-on-year in June to just 29.27 million tons (7.12 million bpd), according to official customs data released Tuesday — the lowest level since October 2016. The figure represents a further 12% decline from May, which had already been an eight-year low.

Chinese refinery run rates slipped to an estimated 57.72% in June, down 3.28 percentage points from May, consultancy Oilchem reported, as high feedstock costs and export restrictions on fuels squeezed margins. Some Chinese refiners have stopped nominating term cargoes from Saudi Arabia for August loading, while others received no term supply allocation at all, according to Bloomberg. Saudi allocations to China have fallen to between 10-20 million barrels per month, down from about 40 million barrels on average last year.

Saudi Arabia has responded by slashing its official selling price for Arab Light loading to Asia in August to $1.50/bbl below the Oman/Dubai benchmark — the deepest discount in two decades. Despite the rare discounting, Gulf competitors offering even bigger discounts and Hormuz-avoiding routes are undercutting Riyadh's position in the Chinese market.
sections.whyItMatters: China's drawdown is the clearest signal yet that the Hormuz disruption is reshaping global trade flows, not just prices. When the world's top crude importer cuts purchases by 40%, it forces a wholesale reorganization of where oil goes and at what price — and Saudi Arabia's record discounting shows it is fighting a losing battle for market share.

sections.heading: Asian Buyers Pivot to US Crude as Iran Sneaks 12 Million Barrels Past Blockade
sections.body:
Asian refiners have restarted negotiations for US spot crude cargoes after weeks of hoping for a Hormuz recovery that never materialized, according to Bloomberg. At least three executives and traders confirmed they had relaunched talks on American spot cargoes, reversing the pause that followed the brief ceasefire.

US crude exports hit a record 5.6 million bpd in April, a 21% increase over the previous all-time high set in December 2023, according to EIA data. The US has become the swing supplier for Asian importers who can no longer rely on Gulf crude passing through Hormuz at predictable rates.

Meanwhile, Iran moved an estimated 12 million barrels of crude through the Strait of Hormuz in the week between the end of the US waiver (July 7) and the reinstatement of the naval blockade (July 14), according to Bloomberg vessel-tracking data. Six sanctioned supertankers transited the Strait outbound with transponders switched off. In total, Iran likely moved 57 million barrels out of the Persian Gulf during the month between the two blockades, with an estimated 50 million barrels of crude and products still stuck in floating storage now that the US blockade has resumed.

Iranian Oil Minister Mohsen Paknejad said Tuesday that 'Iran's oil exports will continue at the same pace as before' despite the canceled waiver, claiming Iran had spent years building sanctions-evasion mechanisms. Nine Iranian tankers went dark off Malaysia carrying crude valued at an estimated $989 million, according to Vortexa and maritime intelligence firm Windward, predominantly bound for Shandong teapot refineries via the established Iran-to-Malaysian-blend-to-China laundering route.
sections.whyItMatters: The juxtaposition is telling: legal trade is reorganizing around the US as swing supplier, while Iran's illicit trade routes remain deeply entrenched. The 12 million barrels that slipped through in one week underlines the limits of naval enforcement, while the pivot to US crude shows how the Hormuz crisis is creating lasting structural winners — chief among them, US shale producers.

sections.heading: Russia-Ukraine Black Sea Attacks Hit Commercial Shipping
sections.body:
A Russian drone strike on a Togo-flagged general cargo ship unloading fertilizer in Odesa killed five seafarers and injured 12 others, according to Odesa authorities. The Russian defense ministry claimed the strikes targeted military cargo. It was one of the deadliest single attacks on commercial shipping since the start of the Ukraine war.

The strike forced major agricultural company Kernel to temporarily suspend operations at nearby Chornomorsk ports. Relentless Russian drone and missile attacks hit Odesa ports over a three-day period, killing port workers, drivers, and sailors.

On the Ukrainian side, forces reported striking 15 Russian vessels overnight in the Sea of Azov and Taganrog Bay, bringing their total to over 105 targeted ships within an eight-day window. The targets included tankers and cargo ships suspected of belonging to Russia's shadow fleet or transporting looted Ukrainian grain and fuel. The Black Sea escalation compounds the Middle East disruption, creating a rare dual-chokepoint risk environment for global energy and commodity shipping.
sections.whyItMatters: The Black Sea is not the Strait of Hormuz in volume terms, but it is critical for Ukrainian grain exports and Russian fuel trade. When both the Black Sea and Hormuz are active military zones simultaneously, global shipping insurance costs rise across all routes, and the risk premium becomes structural rather than localized.

sections.heading: France Cuts 6.4 GW of Nuclear Output as Heatwave Strains European Power
sections.body:
France curtailed 6.4 GW of nuclear power generation on Monday as a prolonged heatwave pushed river temperatures above cooling limits, forcing output reductions at eight reactors. The curtailed output was equivalent to 14% of France's total power demand at the time, according to data from EDF and grid operator RTE cited by Reuters.

Two reactors — Golfech 2 and Bugey 3 — were taken fully offline, while six others operated at reduced rates. Temperatures topped 40°C (104°F) for consecutive days, with red alerts issued throughout France. Thousands of heat-related deaths have been reported since late June.

Despite the nuclear curbs, France remained a net electricity exporter with over 10 GW of power flowing to neighboring countries on Monday. Hydropower generation was also under pressure from the prolonged drought conditions. The Tour de France ran a shortened stage on Sunday for the first time ever due to extreme heat.
sections.whyItMatters: When Europe's largest nuclear fleet loses 14% of national demand at once, it shows the physical limits of even the most robust power systems under climate stress. This is a recurring pattern — French nuclear curtailments during heatwaves are becoming annual — but the scale this time, combined with the energy supply uncertainty from Hormuz, means Europe's power margin is thin in both hydrocarbons and electrons.

sections.heading: DP World Plans Fujairah Mega-Port as Gulf States Build Around Hormuz Permanently
sections.body:
DP World is in talks to build a new multipurpose port and container terminal in Fujairah on the UAE's east coast, creating a shipping route that avoids the Strait of Hormuz entirely, according to the Financial Times. Construction could take as little as 18 months. The announcement comes less than a month after the Strait briefly reopened.

During the Iran war, activity at Dubai's flagship Jebel Ali port collapsed by as much as 95% after Tehran shut the Strait of Hormuz, with cargo shifting east to Fujairah and Khor Fakkan, where congestion quickly followed. The UAE has already been pushing trade east: it pumped a record 4.1 million bpd in June after leaving OPEC, accelerated plans for a second pipeline doubling export capacity from Fujairah, and expanded crude storage at the port.

Gulftainer separately announced a $2 billion expansion at Khor Fakkan. Fujairah already serves as one of the region's largest bunkering hubs and crude storage centers. Pakistan, meanwhile, issued a second emergency LNG spot tender in as many weeks after the latest Hormuz escalation canceled one Qatari cargo delivery, with no LNG tankers observed exiting the Strait for days.
sections.whyItMatters: The 18-month construction timeline for a Hormuz-bypass port says more about Gulf state thinking than any diplomatic statement. Dubai is not planning for the current crisis — it is planning for the next one. The permanent shift of trade infrastructure to the UAE's east coast is the most concrete sign yet that Gulf states view Hormuz vulnerability as a structural, not temporary, problem.

sections.heading: Dangote Refinery Switches to Dollar Pricing, Nigeria Hits Six-Year Output High
sections.body:
Africa's largest refinery, Dangote Petroleum Refinery, has switched domestic gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel pricing to US dollars after struggling to secure enough Nigerian crude through the government's naira-for-crude program. The 650,000 bpd-capacity refinery says it needs 13-15 crude cargoes per month but received only seven from NNPC in May, forcing it to buy the balance on the international market where crude is priced in dollars.

The move effectively ends Nigeria's naira-for-crude experiment, designed to reduce foreign exchange pressure. Every swing in the naira-dollar exchange rate now passes more directly into wholesale fuel prices.

Separately, Nigeria's crude production hit a six-year high of 1.56 million bpd in June, reaching 104% of its OPEC+ quota, according to the NUPRC. Total crude and condensate output hit 1.735 million bpd, with peak production touching 1.89 million bpd. Nigerian authorities are targeting an immediate 100,000 bpd increase to capture supply gaps from the Middle East disruption, with NNPC aiming for 2 million bpd within two years.
sections.whyItMatters: The paradox of Africa's largest refinery pricing fuel in dollars while the country producing the crude can't supply it reveals a structural failure in Nigeria's domestic oil market. But the production surge to a six-year high shows Nigeria positioning itself as a Hormuz alternative supplier — if it can fix its own supply chain first.

bottomLine: Oil prices surged 15% in a week as military escalation hit two major energy shipping routes simultaneously — Hormuz and the Black Sea — for the first time at this intensity since the Iran war began. China's crude imports crashed to a decade low, Asian buyers pivoted back to US crude, and Iran slipped 12 million barrels past the renewed blockade, all confirming that the Hormuz crisis is now reshaping trade flows structurally rather than temporarily. DP World's plan to build a Hormuz-bypass port in Fujairah within 18 months signals that Gulf states are designing for permanent disruption. Meanwhile, France's 6.4 GW nuclear cut during a heatwave and Dangote's switch to dollar pricing show that energy system stress is hitting from every direction — military, climatic, and financial — at once.
sources: OilPrice.com, Bloomberg, Reuters, Al Jazeera, EIA, NUPRC Nigeria, EDF/RTE France, GasBuddy, Vortexa, Windward, TankerTrackers.com, Oilchem, Financial Times, AAA
